2026 NBA Draft

Big Board

My Draft Philosophy

My Philosophy

My Draft Philosophy

My NBA draft philosophy is built around scalability and feel. The NBA is currently structured in a way that if you are not an elite advantage creator offensively, then quick decision-making, physicality, feel, and court mapping are crucial. As usage toggles up, the player must be able to drive efficient offense. On defense, rim protection has never been more important, and team defense is at an all-time high in terms of importance. I really only care about a player being able to positively affect an NBA playoff series. I do take into account that certain players, while not my cup of tea, do have higher ceilings and are worth a risk at a certain point for certain teams. Although I see the allure of the recent uptick in sheets draft philosophy and have definitely utilized certain metrics, I do believe in the importance of watching film and understanding how certain on-court behaviors and skills will translate to not only the NBA, but also the NBA playoffs.

For reference, here is my draft board from last year.

View this post on X →

High Level Playoff Impact Players

Yaxel Lendeborg · Bennet Stirtz · Kingston Flemings

This "tier" has three players I view as being able to impact a playoff series. Flemings and Stirtz have on-and-off-ball court mapping, and Yaxel's physical profile coupled with his willingness to fit into a role lead me to believe that all three will affect a playoff series.

Yaxel has the best blend of team and isolation defense in this class; he will be a day one impact defender in the NBA. His weak-side rim protection and shot blocking remind me a lot of Aaron Gordon with a more big-like effect. His POA defense shined all year. Michigan used him as the primary defender on multiple NBA-level guards, highlighting his lateral quickness and length. Offensively, Yaxel plays a little upright for my liking and I don't think he'll be a high level shooter. His jack-of-all-trades offensive game projects to still add value: passable shooting from a standstill but not good enough to be a pull-up threat or movement shooter, willing passing with decent vision which won't be highlighted with on-ball reps but more so in the short roll and in split-second decisions after an advantage has been created by others, average ball handling and rim finishing that will be better in transition and against a tilted defense. His biggest value-add offensively is the pace and intensity he does all of this at.

We have really only seen Stirtz in a heliocentric role. I may be wrong, but scaling down his offensive load and having him toggle on and off ball should be a somewhat seamless transition. His C&S numbers are good and he makes smart quick decisions in dribble, pass, shoot scenarios. Defensively, I don't think he'll ever be a positive, but he's a competitor and sneaky athletic - his feel and willingness to try hard should carry over to a neutral outlook here. I may be too high on him, but Stirtz does all of the things I want my secondary ball handler to do.

Houston ran their entire offense through Flemings, which I don't believe is his highest and best use in the NBA due to his size. His height and wingspan really cap his offensive ceiling. His quickness and speed allow paint touches and rim pressure, but I fear his finishing ceiling is capped due to his size. This inherently affects his passing: if he's getting into the paint but not scoring efficiently, then collapsing the defense will become less likely due to defenders not feeling the need to fully rotate. This conundrum then starts to discount his passing vision. In my opinion, he needs to play next to a larger creator, e.g. Cade, Luka, Shai, Anthony Edwards. This would allow attacking more on the second side. I'm afraid there is no team in his draft range that will be a seamless fit, but in the right ecosystem Flemings will thrive.

Boom or Bust Primary Initiators

Mikel Brown · Keaton Wagler · Darius Acuff

I view all of these guards in the same boat whereas they have a high lead guard ceiling but the ancillaries and low end outcomes are scary. What brings Mikel to the top of this group for me is the mix of pull up threes and passing could peak at a lead playoff initiator. I hate taking shots on prospects whose only high level playoff outcome requires high usage, but there is an outcome where Mikel is able to demand high usage and bend defenses with shooting and playmaking.

Acuff's body control and just overall ability to create offense are super impressive, but I'm not sure having an offensive system built around him in the NBA leads to a high level playoff team due to my projection of his rim creation and lack of ability to draw two to the ball (lack of overall offensive gravity). I just don't see a path to a top-50 NBA player without being a primary playoff offensive option coupled with his negative defense.

Wagler is interesting because he projects better off ball than Acuff and Brown. His ability to make quick decisions and not hold onto the ball, which seems unimportant and boring, is extremely important in an NBA context. C&S threes and not letting the ball sit in his hands will go a long ways in a playoff offense. While Wagler is taller than Acuff and Brown, his wingspan and weight leave a lot to be desired; his strength is also not very functional which shows up in all avenues defensively and with his driving. Offensively, his core strength and balance do not warrant the same respect as other pace-oriented drivers like Luka, Shai, Brunson, or even Austin Reeves. Better offensive feel, similar defensive impact, and worse on-ball scoring than his peers keep Wagler in this tier.

Positive Role Players

Aday Mara · Morez Johnson · Allen Graves · Brayden Burries

This tier is full of guys I view to have playoff-level attributes and will most likely be positive on both ends of the court. Mara is a little clunky and heavy-footed for my liking, but his sheer size and vertical presence are enough to project him as somewhat productive. He'll be a purely drop big defensively. Being a pure drop big isn't a huge issue, but he's not scheme-resistant, and depending on matchup, he may fare better in the regular season. While Mara's passing is only a positive, I see it being difficult to fully maximize this attribute. Passing as a big is most effective out of the short roll or if they're skilled enough as a scorer to warrant higher usage. Mara isn't nimble enough to operate as a true short-roll threat, and his ball skills aren't great enough to warrant offensive usage.

Morez Johnson has one of the best motors I've seen out of a prospect in years. His ability to affect a game with rebounding and straight-up hustle is pretty impressive. For a playoff offense, I do think Morez will be a clunky fit as he's not great in half-court settings - his offensive rebounding will make up for this to some degree, but having your 4 in the dunker spot isn't ideal on most teams. If Morez's shooting pans out with not only efficiency but volume, then his offense is a different story, but I project low-volume 3PA and a decline in efficiency in a playoff setting.

Burries was, oddly enough, one of the tougher evals for me. NBA teams seem to love him, and I'm usually super high on high-floor guys. Burries worries me, though, as his combined level of handle, court mapping, and athleticism isn't good enough to warrant on-ball reps and may not be good enough to really push value on the second side either. A lot of his points this past year were in transition and in ball-screen actions. This is worrisome, as he's not going to be involved in nearly as many ball screens in the NBA, and his transition opportunities will plummet. This leads to a scenario where his shooting and defense will need to drive a large amount of his value - neither of which is elite. I don't think any player needs an elite skill to make an impact, but when I factor in Burries' age and physical profile, I'm projecting a positive-impact rotational guard but not a needle mover in a playoff series.

Allen Graves is another tough eval. I'm a sucker for great team defenders, and Graves is just that, but something feels a little off with his overall game. On offense, a lot of his touches were through post-ups. These post-ups highlighted his passing and accounted for a good portion of his scoring opportunities. This worries me, as no NBA team is going to run offense through Graves, and his shot diet will need to be purely play-finishing. Graves' shooting projects positive in this regard, as he's strictly a stationary spot-up shooter. Schematically speaking, I do not know where Graves fits in an NBA offense, but I'm willing to bet on the role-player mentality that I think he'll buy into coupled with his defensive feel.

Glaring Weakness with a Glaring Strength

Dailyn Swain · Egbuka Okorie · Hannes Steinbach · Jayden Quaintance · Christian Anderson · Labaron Philon

There comes a point where the value of a player's on-ball creation just doesn't warrant the usage or shot diet in the NBA that they received in college - this sums up my opinion on Swain, Okorie, and Philon. I've been up and down on Swain. The self-creation and paint touches are impressive, but is this really a guy I want handling the ball for my NBA offense? The shooting touch scares me, as his driving will be much less effective if defenders can load up on it and not close out hard. I'd prefer if Swain projected to be a better shooter.

Okorie is another guy I've been up and down on. Like Swain, how much of an NBA offense do you really want to run through a 6'1 guard with average passing vision? The rim pressure is tremendous, but I project Okorie as more of a third guard. I am much lower on Labaron Philon than a lot of my peers. Dillingham burned me in 2024, and I am out on super-low-BMI guards without outlier athleticism.

Hannes Steinbach does not fit what I look for in bigs when projecting playoff performance. He lacks the movement skills and shot blocking to offer much versatility in defending ball screens, which caps his defensive ceiling as a slight positive in my opinion. The rebounding is a saving grace; when coupled with his sheer size and strength, I do think he can carve out minutes in the playoffs.

Built for one person's eyes (and bragging rights) — rankings subject to change daily.
